quiz_questions.question_text: What is a stem cell?

options.text: A fully specialized cell that can no longer divide (like a red blood cell)
A dead cell that is being recycled by the body
A cancer cell that divides uncontrollably
An undifferentiated cell that can both self-renew (divide to make more stem cells) and differentiate into various specialized cell types

quiz_questions.explanation: Stem cells have two defining properties: (1) self-renewal — the ability to divide and produce more stem cells, maintaining the stem cell pool, and (2) potency — the ability to differentiate into one or more specialized cell types. These properties make them fundamentally different from most body cells, which are terminally differentiated and perform specific functions but cannot produce new cell types.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between totipotent, pluripotent, and multipotent stem cells?

options.text: Multipotent has the broadest potential; totipotent has the narrowest

Totipotent can become ANY cell type including placenta (zygote); pluripotent can become almost any body cell type but not placenta (embryonic stem cells); multipotent can become several related cell types (adult stem cells)
These terms refer to different sizes of stem cells, not their potential
All three types have identical differentiation potential

quiz_questions.explanation: Stem cell potency decreases as development progresses. Totipotent cells (the zygote and first few divisions) can form an entire organism including extraembryonic tissues (placenta). Pluripotent cells (inner cell mass of blastocyst → embryonic stem cells) can become any of the ~200+ cell types in the body but not placenta. Multipotent cells (adult stem cells, like hematopoietic stem cells in bone marrow) can only differentiate into a limited range of related cell types within their tissue lineage.

quiz_questions.question_text: Where do embryonic stem cells come from, and why are they scientifically valuable?

options.text: From adult muscle tissue; they can regenerate any organ
From adult bone marrow; they can only produce blood cells
From the inner cell mass of a blastocyst (early embryo, ~5 days after fertilization); they are pluripotent and can differentiate into virtually any cell type in the body
From the placenta; they can only form skin cells

quiz_questions.explanation: Embryonic stem cells (ESCs) are derived from the inner cell mass (ICM) of a blastocyst — a hollow ball of about 100-200 cells formed ~5 days after fertilization. The ICM normally develops into the embryo proper. ESCs are pluripotent: when cultured in a lab, they can be directed to become neurons, heart muscle cells, pancreatic beta cells, blood cells, and virtually any other cell type. This makes them invaluable for research and potentially for cell replacement therapies.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are adult (somatic) stem cells, and where are they found?

options.text: Adult stem cells are multipotent cells found in various tissues (bone marrow, skin, intestines, brain) that maintain and repair those tissues throughout life
Adult stem cells exist only in the blood
Adult stem cells can become any cell type in the body (pluripotent)
Adult stem cells are only found in embryos

quiz_questions.explanation: Adult (somatic) stem cells are undifferentiated cells found in small numbers within differentiated tissues throughout the body. They are typically multipotent — limited to producing cell types within their tissue of origin. Key examples: hematopoietic stem cells (bone marrow → all blood cell types), intestinal stem cells (crypt base → absorptive and secretory gut cells), neural stem cells (brain → neurons, astrocytes, oligodendrocytes), and epidermal stem cells (skin basal layer → keratinocytes). They play essential roles in tissue maintenance and repair.

quiz_questions.question_text: What are induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs), and how are they created?

options.text: iPSCs are genetically modified bacteria used for tissue engineering
iPSCs are stem cells taken from umbilical cord blood
iPSCs are embryonic cells extracted earlier than normal ESCs
iPSCs are adult somatic cells (like skin cells) that have been reprogrammed back to a pluripotent state by introducing specific transcription factors (Oct4, Sox2, Klf4, c-Myc — the Yamanaka factors)

quiz_questions.explanation: Induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) were developed by Shinya Yamanaka in 2006 (Nobel Prize 2012). By introducing four transcription factors (Oct4, Sox2, Klf4, and c-Myc) into adult somatic cells (like skin fibroblasts), the cells are reprogrammed to a pluripotent state resembling embryonic stem cells. iPSCs can differentiate into virtually any cell type. This breakthrough avoids the ethical concerns of destroying embryos and allows patient-specific pluripotent cells to be created, reducing immune rejection risk in therapies.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is cellular differentiation?

options.text: The process of DNA replication during S phase
The process by which an unspecialized cell becomes a specialized cell type with a specific structure and function — driven by selective gene expression
The process by which cells lose their DNA and die
The process by which cells divide to produce identical copies

quiz_questions.explanation: Differentiation is the developmental process where a cell progressively specializes — activating certain genes and silencing others — to acquire the specific structure and function of a mature cell type. All cells in an organism contain the same DNA, but different cell types express different subsets of genes. For example, a stem cell might differentiate into a muscle cell (expressing actin, myosin) or a neuron (expressing ion channels, neurotransmitter receptors). Differentiation is usually irreversible under normal conditions, though iPSC technology reverses it artificially.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do bone marrow transplants use stem cells to treat disease?

options.text: Bone marrow transplants replace damaged bone tissue with healthy bone
Bone marrow transplants provide temporary pain relief only
Neural stem cells from bone marrow repair brain damage
Donated hematopoietic stem cells from bone marrow engraft in the patient's marrow and regenerate all blood cell types — used to treat leukemia, lymphoma, sickle cell disease, and immune disorders

quiz_questions.explanation: Bone marrow transplants (hematopoietic stem cell transplants) are the most established stem cell therapy. The patient's diseased or damaged bone marrow is destroyed with chemotherapy/radiation, then replaced with healthy hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) from a donor's bone marrow, peripheral blood, or umbilical cord blood. The transplanted HSCs migrate to the bone marrow space, engraft, and regenerate the entire blood system — red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets. This can cure leukemia, sickle cell disease, and severe immune deficiencies.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is regenerative medicine?

options.text: Surgery to remove damaged organs without replacing them
A field focused on replacing organs with mechanical devices only
Traditional medicine using herbal remedies
A field of medicine that aims to repair or replace damaged tissues and organs using stem cells, tissue engineering, biomaterials, and growth factors to restore normal function

quiz_questions.explanation: Regenerative medicine is an interdisciplinary field that combines stem cell biology, tissue engineering, biomaterials science, and molecular biology to develop therapies that repair, replace, or regenerate damaged or diseased tissues and organs. Approaches include: stem cell transplantation (injecting cells to repair tissue), tissue engineering (growing tissues on biodegradable scaffolds), 3D bioprinting (layer-by-layer construction of tissue), and gene therapy (correcting genetic defects in a patient's own cells). The ultimate goal is to restore normal tissue function.

quiz_questions.question_text: What ethical concerns surround embryonic stem cell research?

options.text: There are no ethical concerns — everyone agrees ESC research is acceptable
The only concern is the financial cost of the research
The primary ethical concern is that obtaining ESCs requires the destruction of human embryos (blastocysts), raising questions about the moral status of early embryos
ESC research is unethical because the cells don't work

quiz_questions.explanation: The central ethical debate concerns the moral status of human embryos. Obtaining ESCs from the inner cell mass of a blastocyst destroys the embryo, which some consider the end of a potential human life. Views vary widely based on religious, philosophical, and cultural beliefs about when life begins. iPSC technology (reprogramming adult cells to pluripotency) partially addresses this concern by avoiding embryo destruction. Other ethical issues include informed consent for embryo donation, commercialization risks, and equitable access to stem cell therapies.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is somatic cell nuclear transfer (SCNT), and what was its most famous application?

options.text: SCNT is artificial insemination used in livestock breeding
SCNT is a technique that extracts DNA from fossils to clone extinct animals

SCNT transfers the nucleus of a somatic (body) cell into an enucleated egg cell, creating an embryo genetically identical to the donor — famously used to create Dolly the sheep (1996), the first mammal cloned from an adult cell
SCNT is gene therapy that inserts new genes into stem cells

quiz_questions.explanation: Somatic cell nuclear transfer (SCNT) involves removing the nucleus from an egg cell and replacing it with the nucleus from an adult somatic cell. The reconstructed egg is stimulated to begin dividing, creating an embryo that is a genetic clone of the nuclear donor. In 1996, Ian Wilmut used SCNT to create Dolly the sheep from a mammary gland cell — proving that differentiated adult cells retain all genetic information needed to produce a complete organism. SCNT is used in research to produce patient-matched ESCs (therapeutic cloning) without requiring fertilization.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'Stem Cell'?

options.text: A cell found only in plant meristems, not in animals
An undifferentiated cell capable of self-renewal (dividing to produce more stem cells) and differentiation (becoming specialized cell types), serving as the body's internal repair system
A cancer cell that has lost all growth control
A fully differentiated cell that performs a single specialized function and can never divide again

quiz_questions.explanation: A stem cell is defined by two key properties: (1) self-renewal — the ability to undergo cell division and produce daughter cells that remain stem cells, maintaining the stem cell population, and (2) potency — the ability to differentiate into one or more specialized cell types. Stem cells range from totipotent (can form an entire organism) to pluripotent (can form any body cell type) to multipotent (limited to related cell types within a tissue). They serve as the body's repair and regeneration system, replacing cells lost through normal wear, injury, or disease.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'Embryonic Stem Cell'?

options.text: A cell that can only form extraembryonic tissues like the placenta
A pluripotent cell derived from the inner cell mass of a blastocyst (~5 days post-fertilization) that can differentiate into virtually any cell type in the body
A multipotent cell found in adult tissues that can only produce cell types within its lineage
A somatic cell that has been reprogrammed using Yamanaka factors

quiz_questions.explanation: Embryonic stem cells (ESCs) are pluripotent cells isolated from the inner cell mass (ICM) of a blastocyst — the hollow ball of cells that forms about 5 days after fertilization. The ICM normally gives rise to the embryo proper (all three germ layers: ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm). In culture, ESCs can be maintained indefinitely while retaining pluripotency, and can be directed to differentiate into neurons, cardiomyocytes, blood cells, pancreatic beta cells, and essentially any cell type of the body. Their isolation requires destruction of the blastocyst, which raises ethical concerns.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'Adult Stem Cell'?

options.text: A pluripotent cell that can form any cell type, equivalent in potency to embryonic stem cells

A multipotent, tissue-resident stem cell found in differentiated organs (bone marrow, skin, gut, brain) that maintains and repairs the tissue by producing a limited range of specialized cell types within its lineage
A cell that exists only in adults over 18 years old
A terminally differentiated cell that has completed its development

quiz_questions.explanation: Adult (somatic) stem cells are undifferentiated cells found in small numbers within mature, differentiated tissues throughout life — even in children and infants, despite the name 'adult.' They are typically multipotent, meaning they can produce several related cell types but not all body cell types. Examples: hematopoietic stem cells (bone marrow → red/white blood cells, platelets), intestinal stem cells (gut crypts → absorptive/secretory cells), neural stem cells (brain → neurons, glia), and mesenchymal stem cells (bone marrow → bone, cartilage, fat). They are the body's built-in repair crew for tissue homeostasis.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'Regenerative Medicine'?

options.text: A field of medicine focused exclusively on organ transplantation from donors
Traditional herbal medicine practices used for wound healing
The study of how amphibians naturally regrow lost limbs, with no human application

An interdisciplinary field that uses stem cells, tissue engineering, biomaterials, and growth factors to repair, replace, or regenerate damaged or diseased cells, tissues, and organs to restore normal function

quiz_questions.explanation: Regenerative medicine is a broad interdisciplinary field at the intersection of biology, engineering, and clinical medicine. Its goal is to develop therapies that restore the structure and function of damaged tissues and organs rather than merely managing symptoms. Key approaches include: stem cell transplantation (injecting cells to replace damaged tissue), tissue engineering (growing tissues on biodegradable scaffolds seeded with cells), 3D bioprinting (precisely depositing cells and biomaterials layer by layer), and gene therapy (correcting genetic defects in patient cells). It holds promise for conditions currently treatable only by organ transplant.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'Differentiation'?

options.text: The death of a cell through programmed apoptosis
The process of DNA replication during S phase of the cell cycle
The developmental process by which an unspecialized (stem) cell becomes a specialized cell type with a distinct structure and function, driven by selective activation and silencing of specific genes
The process of cell division that produces genetically identical daughter cells

quiz_questions.explanation: Differentiation is the process by which a less specialized cell becomes a more specialized cell type. All cells in an organism contain the same DNA, but different cell types express different subsets of genes — controlled by transcription factors, epigenetic modifications, and signaling pathways. As a stem cell differentiates, it progressively activates genes needed for its target function (e.g., hemoglobin genes in red blood cells, keratin in skin cells) while silencing others. Differentiation is normally irreversible, though iPSC technology artificially reverses it by reactivating pluripotency genes.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'Totipotent'?

options.text: A cell with the highest potency — capable of forming ALL cell types of the organism PLUS extraembryonic tissues (placenta), a property limited to the zygote and first few cell divisions
A cell that has permanently lost the ability to divide
A cell that can become any body cell type but not placental tissue
A cell that can become several related cell types within one tissue lineage

quiz_questions.explanation: Totipotent (Latin: totus = whole, potens = powerful) describes cells with the broadest developmental potential — they can give rise to every cell type needed for a complete organism, including all embryonic cell types AND extraembryonic structures like the placenta, umbilical cord, and amniotic membranes. Only the zygote and cells from the first ~3-4 cell divisions (up to about the 16-cell morula stage) are truly totipotent. After that, cells begin to specialize and lose totipotency, becoming pluripotent in the inner cell mass of the blastocyst.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'Pluripotent'?

options.text: A cell capable of differentiating into virtually any cell type of the body (all three germ layers: ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm) but NOT extraembryonic tissues — characteristic of embryonic stem cells and iPSCs
A cell that can form an entire organism including the placenta
A cell restricted to producing only blood cell types
A cell that can only become one specific cell type

quiz_questions.explanation: Pluripotent (Latin: pluris = many) cells can differentiate into any of the ~200+ specialized cell types derived from the three embryonic germ layers — ectoderm (skin, neurons), mesoderm (muscle, bone, blood), and endoderm (gut lining, lungs, liver) — but cannot form extraembryonic tissues (placenta). Embryonic stem cells (from the blastocyst ICM) and induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs, reprogrammed adult cells) are both pluripotent. Pluripotency is one step below totipotency in the potency hierarchy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'Multipotent'?

options.text: A cell that can become any cell type in the body
A stem cell that can differentiate into several related cell types within a particular tissue lineage, but not all body cell types — characteristic of most adult stem cells
A cell that has completely lost the ability to differentiate
A cell that can form an entire organism plus placenta

quiz_questions.explanation: Multipotent (Latin: multus = many) describes stem cells with more restricted potency than pluripotent cells — they can produce multiple cell types but only within a related lineage. Most adult stem cells are multipotent. For example, hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) in bone marrow can differentiate into red blood cells, white blood cells (neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes), and platelets — all blood cell types — but cannot become neurons, muscle cells, or skin cells. Neural stem cells can produce neurons, astrocytes, and oligodendrocytes but not blood cells.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'Induced Pluripotent Stem Cell (iPSC)'?

options.text: An adult somatic cell (e.g., skin fibroblast) that has been artificially reprogrammed to a pluripotent state by introducing specific transcription factors (Oct4, Sox2, Klf4, c-Myc — the Yamanaka factors), giving it ESC-like differentiation potential without embryo destruction
An embryonic stem cell that has been genetically modified to resist immune rejection
A stem cell created by somatic cell nuclear transfer (cloning)
A naturally occurring pluripotent cell found in adult bone marrow

quiz_questions.explanation: Induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) were pioneered by Shinya Yamanaka in 2006 (Nobel Prize 2012). By delivering four transcription factors — Oct4, Sox2, Klf4, and c-Myc (the 'Yamanaka factors') — into adult somatic cells (typically skin fibroblasts or blood cells), the cells are reprogrammed to a pluripotent state functionally equivalent to embryonic stem cells. iPSCs can differentiate into virtually any cell type. This technology avoids the ethical issues of embryo destruction and enables patient-specific cells, reducing immune rejection risk. It opened entirely new avenues for disease modeling, drug testing, and personalized regenerative therapy.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes 'Somatic Cell Nuclear Transfer (SCNT)'?

options.text: Gene therapy that corrects mutations in stem cells
The natural process of fertilization where sperm and egg nuclei fuse

A cloning technique in which the nucleus of an adult somatic cell is transferred into an enucleated egg cell, producing an embryo genetically identical to the nuclear donor — used to create Dolly the sheep (1996) and for therapeutic cloning to generate patient-matched ESCs
A technique that introduces transcription factors into cells to reprogram them

quiz_questions.explanation: Somatic cell nuclear transfer (SCNT) is a laboratory technique where the nucleus (containing DNA) is removed from an egg cell and replaced with the nucleus from an adult body (somatic) cell. The reconstructed egg is activated (chemically or electrically) to begin dividing, producing an embryo that is a genetic clone of the nuclear donor. SCNT achieved worldwide fame when Ian Wilmut and Keith Campbell used it in 1996 to create Dolly the sheep — the first mammal cloned from an adult somatic cell. In medicine, therapeutic cloning uses SCNT to create blastocysts from which patient-matched ESCs can be derived, avoiding immune rejection.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 62-year-old patient with Parkinson's disease has progressive loss of dopamine-producing neurons in the substantia nigra. Researchers propose transplanting iPSC-derived dopaminergic neurons into the patient's brain. Why might iPSCs be preferred over embryonic stem cells for this patient?

options.text: iPSCs can be derived from the patient's own skin cells, making the transplanted neurons genetically identical to the patient and greatly reducing immune rejection risk — while also avoiding the ethical issues of embryo destruction
iPSCs are totipotent while ESCs are only multipotent
iPSCs do not require any laboratory manipulation
iPSCs divide faster than ESCs

quiz_questions.explanation: iPSCs offer two major advantages for this Parkinson's patient: (1) Autologous therapy — iPSCs can be made from the patient's own somatic cells (skin fibroblasts or blood cells), reprogrammed using Yamanaka factors, then differentiated into dopaminergic neurons. Because these neurons are genetically identical to the patient, the immune system is far less likely to reject them, potentially eliminating the need for lifelong immunosuppressive drugs. (2) Ethical advantage — no embryo destruction is required. Clinical trials (e.g., by Jun Takahashi's team in Japan) have begun transplanting iPSC-derived dopaminergic progenitors into Parkinson's patients.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 5-year-old child with severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID, 'bubble boy disease') receives a bone marrow transplant from an HLA-matched sibling donor. Six months later, blood tests show the child is producing normal levels of all white blood cell types. What explains this recovery?

options.text: The donor's pluripotent stem cells formed new organs in the child
The transplant provided mature white blood cells that will last a lifetime
The child's own stem cells were reprogrammed by the transplant

The transplanted hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) engrafted in the patient's bone marrow and, being multipotent, differentiated into all blood cell lineages — including the functional lymphocytes (T cells, B cells, NK cells) the child's own marrow could not produce

quiz_questions.explanation: Bone marrow transplant is the classic stem cell therapy. The donated HSCs migrate to and colonize the patient's bone marrow (engraftment), where they self-renew and differentiate into all blood cell lineages: erythrocytes (red blood cells), platelets, and — critically for SCID — all types of leukocytes including T lymphocytes, B lymphocytes, and NK cells. Because HSCs are multipotent and self-renewing, a single successful engraftment provides a permanent, self-sustaining source of functional immune cells. HLA matching between donor and recipient minimizes graft-versus-host disease (GvHD).

quiz_questions.question_text: A biotech company uses Yamanaka factors (Oct4, Sox2, Klf4, c-Myc) to reprogram adult skin fibroblasts into iPSCs, then differentiates them into cardiomyocytes (heart muscle cells) for drug toxicity testing. Why is this approach valuable for pharmaceutical development?

options.text: iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes are cheaper than any alternative testing method
Drug testing on iPSC cells eliminates all need for clinical trials

It allows testing drug effects on functional human heart cells in a dish — including patient-specific cells that can reveal individual variations in drug response — without the ethical and practical limitations of testing directly on human hearts or relying solely on animal models
The iPSC cardiomyocytes are identical to animal heart cells

quiz_questions.explanation: iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes have transformed drug safety testing. Many drugs fail in late-stage clinical trials because they cause cardiac toxicity (arrhythmias, QT prolongation) that animal models don't predict well. iPSC-cardiomyocytes allow pharmaceutical companies to: (1) test drugs on functional human heart cells that beat and conduct electrical signals, (2) create patient-specific cardiomyocytes to identify genetic susceptibility to drug side effects (pharmacogenomics), and (3) screen thousands of compounds efficiently. This approach has detected cardiotoxicity earlier in development, potentially saving billions in failed drug programs and protecting patient safety.

quiz_questions.question_text: A burn victim with 40% total body surface area burns receives a skin graft grown from their own epidermal stem cells. A small biopsy of healthy skin was taken, the stem cells were expanded in culture for 3 weeks, and sheets of new skin were applied to the burns. Why are the patient's own stem cells used rather than donor skin?

options.text: The patient's stem cells are totipotent, while donor cells are only unipotent

Autologous (self-derived) epidermal stem cells produce skin that is genetically identical to the patient, virtually eliminating immune rejection — whereas donor skin (allograft) would be recognized as foreign and attacked by the immune system, requiring immunosuppression
The patient's own skin cells divide faster than donor cells
Donor skin cannot be cultured in a laboratory

quiz_questions.explanation: Autologous stem cell therapy (using the patient's own cells) is preferred because it avoids the #1 challenge of transplantation medicine: immune rejection. The patient's adaptive immune system (T cells) recognizes foreign MHC/HLA molecules on donor cells and mounts an attack, destroying the graft. By culturing the patient's own epidermal stem cells (which are multipotent — producing keratinocytes, melanocytes, and other skin cell types), the resulting tissue is genetically self and is not rejected. This approach, pioneered by Howard Green, has saved lives of severe burn patients by growing enough skin from a small biopsy to cover extensive wounds.

quiz_questions.question_text: Scientists are using 3D bioprinting to construct a kidney scaffold seeded with patient-derived iPSC cells that have been differentiated into nephron-specific cell types. What key challenge must be solved before bioprinted kidneys can replace donor kidneys for transplantation?

options.text: iPSCs cannot be differentiated into kidney cell types

The bioprinted organ must develop a functional vascular network (blood supply) that integrates with the patient's circulatory system — without vascularization, cells deeper than ~200 micrometers from a blood vessel will die from oxygen and nutrient deprivation
3D printers cannot print biological materials at all
Kidneys are too small to benefit from bioprinting technology

quiz_questions.explanation: Vascularization is the most critical unsolved challenge in bioprinting complex organs like kidneys, livers, and hearts. Simple tissues (skin, cartilage) can survive via diffusion because they are thin, but solid organs require an intricate network of blood vessels to deliver oxygen and nutrients to every cell. The kidney has a particularly complex vascular architecture (each kidney contains ~1 million nephrons with elaborate capillary networks). Current 3D bioprinting can create the structural scaffold and seed it with appropriate cells, but engineering a functional, perfusable vascular network that connects to the patient's circulation remains the primary bottleneck.

quiz_questions.question_text: A couple expecting a baby decides to bank their newborn's umbilical cord blood. The marketing materials claim cord blood contains valuable stem cells. What type of stem cells does cord blood contain, and what conditions can they potentially treat?

options.text: Cord blood contains only red blood cells with no stem cell properties
Cord blood stem cells can only treat the baby, never anyone else

Cord blood contains hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs) that can reconstitute the blood and immune system — used to treat leukemia, lymphoma, sickle cell disease, thalassemia, and immune deficiencies, similar to bone marrow transplants
Cord blood contains totipotent stem cells that can form any cell type including organs

quiz_questions.explanation: Umbilical cord blood is a rich, easily accessible source of hematopoietic stem cells (HSCs). These multipotent HSCs can differentiate into all blood cell types and have been used since 1988 to treat blood cancers (leukemia, lymphoma), inherited blood disorders (sickle cell disease, thalassemia), and immune deficiencies. Cord blood HSCs are immunologically naive (less likely to cause graft-versus-host disease) and don't require perfect HLA matching, making them useful when no matched bone marrow donor is available. Both private banking (for family use) and public banking (donated for anyone) are options.

quiz_questions.question_text: Dolly the sheep, cloned via SCNT in 1996, developed arthritis and lung disease and was euthanized at age 6 (sheep typically live ~12 years). Some scientists speculated her premature aging was linked to the fact that the donor nucleus came from a 6-year-old ewe's mammary cell. What cellular factor could explain premature aging in Dolly?

options.text: The donor somatic cell's telomeres were already shortened due to 6 years of cell divisions — when the nucleus was transferred into the enucleated egg, incomplete telomere reprogramming may have given Dolly cells with pre-shortened telomeres, causing them to reach senescence earlier than normal
Dolly had too many chromosomes from the cloning process
Cloned animals cannot produce any immune cells
The egg cell added harmful mutations to the donor DNA

quiz_questions.explanation: Telomere length is a biological clock — telomeres (protective DNA caps on chromosome ends) shorten with each cell division, and when they become critically short, cells enter senescence or die. The mammary cell used to clone Dolly had already undergone 6 years of divisions, shortening its telomeres. While the egg cytoplasm can partially reset epigenetic marks (reprogramming), telomere restoration by telomerase may have been incomplete. This means Dolly's cells may have started life with the biological age of a 6-year-old sheep. This 'telomere problem' is one of several reprogramming challenges in SCNT cloning (though later studies on Dolly's cloned 'sisters' showed healthier aging).

quiz_questions.question_text: A clinical trial uses iPSC-derived retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) cells to treat age-related macular degeneration (AMD), a leading cause of blindness in the elderly. The RPE cells are transplanted beneath the patient's retina. Why is the eye considered an ideal early target for stem cell therapy compared to other organs?

options.text: Eye cells never undergo immune rejection under any circumstances
The eye does not require blood supply, so vascularization is not an issue
RPE cells are the easiest cell type to differentiate from any stem cell source

The eye is an immune-privileged site (limited immune surveillance), requires relatively few transplanted cells, the target cell type (RPE) is a single monolayer, disease progression can be precisely monitored with imaging, and the other eye serves as an internal control

quiz_questions.explanation: The eye has several features that make it an ideal 'proving ground' for stem cell therapies: (1) Immune privilege — the blood-retinal barrier and local immunosuppressive factors limit immune cell access, reducing rejection risk. (2) Small cell requirement — AMD involves loss of RPE, a single-cell-thick layer; only ~100,000-500,000 cells are needed per transplant. (3) Accessible anatomy — cells can be precisely delivered via subretinal injection. (4) Measurable outcomes — OCT imaging and visual acuity tests can objectively track results. (5) Built-in control — the untreated eye provides comparison. The RIKEN Center in Japan and Moorfields Eye Hospital in London have conducted pioneering iPSC-RPE trials.

quiz_questions.question_text: Scientists grow 'mini-brains' (cerebral organoids) in a dish by differentiating iPSCs through stages that recapitulate embryonic brain development. These organoids develop cortical layers, neurons, and glial cells. Researchers use them to study Zika virus infection during brain development. What advantage do organoids offer over traditional 2D cell cultures and animal models?

options.text: Organoids can replace all animal testing immediately

Organoids are 3D structures that self-organize to mimic the architecture and cell-cell interactions of real organs, providing a more physiologically relevant model than flat 2D cultures — while using human cells that better predict human-specific responses than animal models (e.g., Zika targets human neural progenitors differently than mouse progenitors)
Organoids are identical to fully developed human brains
Organoids do not require any stem cells to create

quiz_questions.explanation: Organoids bridge the gap between oversimplified 2D cell cultures and complex (but not always human-relevant) animal models. When iPSCs are cultured in 3D with appropriate growth factors, they self-organize into structures resembling miniature organs — cerebral organoids develop layered cortical tissue, intestinal organoids form crypt-villus structures, and kidney organoids form nephron-like segments. For Zika research, cerebral organoids revealed that the virus preferentially infects human neural progenitor cells, causing microcephaly-like growth reduction — a finding that was difficult to replicate in mice. Organoids also enable personalized medicine: patient-specific organoids can test drug responses for that individual.

quiz_questions.question_text: A research team combines CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing with iPSC technology to treat sickle cell disease. They take skin cells from a patient, reprogram them to iPSCs, use CRISPR to correct the sickle cell mutation (Glu→Val at position 6 of beta-globin), differentiate the corrected iPSCs into hematopoietic stem cells, and transplant them back. What makes this combined approach so powerful?

options.text: iPSCs naturally correct genetic mutations during reprogramming
This approach only treats symptoms and requires lifelong treatment
CRISPR alone can cure sickle cell disease without any stem cell involvement

It combines genetic correction (CRISPR fixes the disease-causing mutation) with cellular therapy (iPSC-derived HSCs provide a self-renewing source of corrected blood cells) in an autologous system (patient's own cells, minimizing immune rejection) — potentially curing a genetic disease at its root cause rather than managing symptoms

quiz_questions.explanation: This ex vivo gene therapy approach represents the cutting edge of regenerative medicine. The workflow exploits three converging technologies: (1) iPSC reprogramming — converting the patient's somatic cells to pluripotent stem cells that can be expanded indefinitely, providing unlimited material. (2) CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing — precisely correcting the single nucleotide mutation (A→T in codon 6 of HBB gene) that causes the Glu→Val substitution creating sickle hemoglobin (HbS). (3) Directed differentiation — converting corrected iPSCs into hematopoietic stem cells that can engraft in bone marrow and produce normal red blood cells with functional hemoglobin (HbA). Because the corrected HSCs self-renew, a single successful transplant could provide a lifelong cure. This paradigm — patient cells → iPSCs → gene correction → differentiation → autologous transplant — is being pursued for sickle cell disease, beta-thalassemia, hemophilia, and other monogenic disorders.
